data: What we observed across all six countries

In all countries, the initial decision to authorize LEO satellite services was made at the high political level by presidents, prime ministers, or senior ministers rather than by independent regulatory bodies. The regulator's role was almost universally to ratify a political decision already taken, often at speed and under conditions that made meaningful scrutiny difficult.

Every country adapted frameworks designed for terrestrial telecoms or geostationary satellites. None had a LEO licensing regime prior to Starlink’s application. Frameworks created in haste, with ambiguous entry conditions, and gaps that became visible only after operators were established and alternatives were limited. Structural gaps exist in terms of market concentration risk, space sustainability, and digital sovereignty.

Across all six countries, governance processes lacked meaningful consultation, transparency, and accountability. In most cases, there was no adequate legal framework. Commercial interests, framed through emergency rhetoric, political timing, digital resilience, or foreign investment, overrode governance norms. Decisions were made at the top and handed down.

Multiple providers are authorized on paper, but Starlink is a dominant provider in practice.

LEO connectivity is promoted as a digital inclusion tool. Its actual affordability for the communities it is supposed to reach is the sharpest and most consistent finding across all six cases. In every country, the cost of LEO satellite hardware and subscriptions significantly exceeds what low-income and rural households can afford without subsidy. Household adoption of LEO connectivity is viable only for a narrow, higher-income segment of the population. So, Starlink moved from “connecting the unconnected” to “connecting those who can pay”.

In all countries, the accountability architecture is weaker than the authorization architecture. Every country authorized Starlink with speed, political intent, or both. None built accountability systems that matched the scale or pace of that authorization.

No country has published the full text of its LEO authorization documents, including all annexes, conditions, and commitments, in a form that allows civil society or researchers to monitor compliance.

Consumer protection mechanisms for LEO satellite services are either nonexistent or not specifically adapted to the sector's characteristics. Standard telecom consumer protection frameworks were designed for terrestrial operators with local presence, predictable pricing models, and national regulatory jurisdiction. LEO operators challenge all three assumptions.
Executive Lobbying and the Quest for Autonomy

Brazil is Starlink's second-largest global market, with 250,000 active users and 85% annual growth in 2025. Despite strong urban penetration, only 22% of Brazilians meet 'meaningful connectivity' standards.
The authorization was actively engineered by the Bolsonaro government, which lobbied the regulator and met directly with SpaceX weeks before the regulatory framework was active.
Starlink operates via Starlink Brazil Holding Ltda. and holds an exploitation right for spectrum and a retail broadband license.
While five providers are authorized on paper, the market is in practice a near-monopoly where Starlink is the only operational consumer LEO player at scale.
The framework initially lacked data localization requirements, space sustainability rules, and mechanisms for state control during geopolitical disputes.
Connectivity as a Disaster Resilience Lifeline

Fiji, an archipelago of 332 islands, views LEO technology as a "game-changer" for its maritime communities. Licensing was framed as a necessity for disaster resilience, particularly given the vulnerability of submarine cables.
LEO licensing was framed as a strategic necessity to provide a disaster-resilient alternative to submarine cables, which are historically vulnerable in the archipelago.
Starlink operates as a registered local entity (Starlink Fiji Pte Limited) and was granted both a commercial and spectrum license to operate.
Although Starlink was initially licensed as a named operator, the government is actively exploring OneWeb and Amazon Kuiper to introduce market diversity and competitive pressure.
There are currently no publicly disclosed quality-of-service standards, mandatory outage reporting, or transparent cybersecurity audit requirements specific to LEO services in Fiji.
The "Red Carpet" and the Local ISP Tension

In 2024, Starlink officially launched in Indonesia following years of personal lobbying by President Joko Widodo. With 12,548 villages still signal blank spots, Starlink has instead entered urban markets where connectivity already exists.
Starlink officially launched in Indonesia following years of personal lobbying by President Joko Widodo.
Starlink established its subsidiary in Indonesia under the name PT Starlink Services Indonesia (SSI) to engage in satellite telecommunications and internet services.
Local industry associations have accused the government of rolling out a "red carpet" for foreign entities while local players face strict regulatory burdens.
Local internet service providers argue that Starlink could put them out of business by offering prices below market standards.
Strategic Alliances and Foreign Ownership Exceptions

Malaysia granted Starlink approval to operate as a 100% foreign-owned entity in July 2023, an exception to the standard 49% foreign equity cap for license holders — following a direct video call between PM Anwar Ibrahim and Elon Musk.
This decision followed a video call between Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim and Elon Musk where both agreed to strengthen the investment relationship.
Malaysia granted Starlink a rare exception to operate as a fully foreign-owned entity despite the standard 49% foreign equity cap.
The local incumbent MEASAT has formed alliances with Chinese LEO firms like Shanghai Spacesail to explore satellite-based IoT.
The country lacks a unified framework integrating privacy, cybersecurity, and competition, leading to an asymmetry between public and private sector accountability.
The Most Advanced Model, the Deepest Affordability Crisis

Over 19,000 terrestrial fiber cuts in early 2025 made satellite connectivity a strategic necessity. Nigeria has since adopted the most advanced sovereignty protections of any studied country — yet hardware costs 4.5 months of minimum wage.
Nigeria transitioned in 2026 from a Starlink-only model to a multi-constellation policy to ensure market resilience and end single-provider dominance.
Nigeria mandates that LEO providers land data at local gateways and form local partnerships to ensure digital sovereignty.
Ending Starlink's single-provider dominance through the issuance of multiple permits has created a diverse and competitive ecosystem.
A key gap is the absence of a local technical presence requirement, leaving rural users without physical support for hardware failures.
Wartime Emergency and the Quest for Autonomy

For Ukraine, LEO satellites are a survival tool. Following the 2022 invasion, Starlink became the backbone of military and civilian resilience — but geofencing restrictions in occupied territories exposed the cost of single-provider dependency.
For Ukraine, LEO satellites are a survival tool that has become the backbone of military and civilian resilience.
In 2026, the Ministry of Defence introduced a terminal verification/whitelist system requiring registration of all operational terminals.
Ukraine is developing UASAT, its first national LEO satellite project, to achieve national autonomy in satellite communications.
Geofencing restrictions and the lack of Starlink access over occupied territories have highlighted the strategic dangers of overreliance on a single foreign provider.
Who controls satellite internet? We’re tracking who profits from connectivity in space and who gets left behind.

LEO satellite governance, Starlink regulation, digital sovereignty, satellite internet policy, orbital commons, satellite market concentration, connectivity divide, spectrum equity, ITU WRC-27, satellite licensing transparency
